paper.title: Dead lithium formation in lithium metal batteries: A phase field model

MaterialSystem.composition: anode=Li metal; electrolyte=liquid electrolyte containing Li ions
MaterialSystem.evidence: In this two-phase system, there is both an electrolyte liquid phase containing lithium ions and a solid phase composed of lithium metal.

Phenomenon.description: Detachment of metallic lithium from bulk active lithium during stripping, producing electronically isolated lithium that cannot contribute capacity.
Phenomenon.evidence: During the stripping process, the lithium dendrites tend to dissolve unevenly, causing some metallic lithium to separate from the bulk lithium metal anode. The detached lithium metal, which is named dead lithium and also the main component of inactive lithium, cannot exchange electrons with the current collector, and is therefore unable to contribute its capacity in the subsequent cycles.

Phenomenon.description: Electrochemical dissolution of lithium metal into lithium ions during discharge, including morphology evolution and dead lithium generation.
Phenomenon.evidence: This work proposed a phase field model to describe the lithium stripping process with dead lithium formation.

Phenomenon.description: Growth of dendritic lithium structures during lithium plating, which provides the initial morphology that leads to dead lithium during stripping.
Phenomenon.evidence: The dendritic lithium plating morphology is the main prerequisite for the massive production of dead lithium in the lithium stripping process.

Assumption.statement: The lithium metal/electrolyte interface is represented as a diffuse numerical interface using a phase-field order parameter.
Assumption.evidence: n varies from 0 to 1 in the interfacial region, representing a diffuse interface between lithium metal and electrolyte. It should be noticed that such diffuse interface is a numerical virtual interface, and does not refer to the actual SEI on lithium metal anodes.

Assumption.statement: The electrostatic potential in the lithium metal solid phase is spatially uniform and evolves only with time.
Assumption.evidence: The electrostatic potential in the lithium metal solid phase us here is considered equal everywhere in the whole lithium metal solid phase, and only evolves over time, which is controlled by galvanostatic simulation conditions.

Assumption.statement: A local lithium region is considered dead when the electrolyte potential exceeds a reference threshold, implemented through an active-state step function.
Assumption.evidence: where fd = fstep(ul/ud) is introduced as a variable to describe the active state of local lithium metal; fstep(x) is a step function that steps from 1 to 0 when x is larger than 1; the reference potential ud is set as 1.0 mV, thus, fd = 1 denotes active lithium, fd = 0 denotes dead lithium.

Assumption.statement: The model is solved on a two-dimensional square domain.
Assumption.evidence: A two-dimensional model consisting of a 30.0 lm × 30.0 lm square domain is introduced.

SimulationModel.objective: Describe lithium stripping with dead lithium formation under galvanostatic conditions and connect morphology evolution to electrochemical performance.
SimulationModel.evidence: In this contribution, a phase field model on the lithium stripping process with dead lithium formation is constructed for the first time. The galvanostatic charge and discharge process of lithium metal anodes were also firstly simulated with phase field models.

SimulationModel.objective: Generate dendritic lithium morphology used as the initial state for stripping simulations.
SimulationModel.evidence: In order to accurately quantitatively simulate the formation process of dead lithium, the lithium dendrite morphology was firstly generated with this galvanostatic lithium plating phase field model.

ModelEquation.description: Total free-energy functional combining chemical, gradient, electrostatic, and noise contributions for the phase-field description.
ModelEquation.evidence: Based on Chen’s nonlinear phase field model and our previous works [49,57,59,63], the total free energy can be given by F = ∫V fch(ci) + fgrad(η) + felec(ci, φ) + fns(η) dV

ModelEquation.description: Phase-field evolution equation for lithium plating coupled with Butler–Volmer kinetics.
ModelEquation.evidence: Coupled with Butler–Volmer kinetics, the partial differential equations that describe the temporal evolution of order parameter n can be deduced [57,59]. For lithium plating process

ModelEquation.description: Phase-field evolution equation for lithium stripping including active/dead lithium state and Butler–Volmer kinetics.
Temporal evolution of Li-ion concentration including diffusion, migration, and interfacial consumption/production.
ModelEquation.evidence: The partial differential equation that describe the temporal evolution of Li ion concentration is given as

ModelEquation.description: Governing equation for the electrolyte liquid-phase electric potential.
ModelEquation.evidence: The electric potential in the electrolyte liquid phase ul can be solved with Poisson’s equation ∇2ul = 0

ModelEquation.description: Global galvanostatic constraint enforcing equality between applied areal current density, reaction current density, and mass-transfer current density.
ModelEquation.evidence: The galvanostatic simulation conditions are realized by introducing global equations, which solve two variables us and ul,up by iterative method to keep the two equal signs in the following equation are always true during the whole lithium plating/stripping process I0 = Ir = Imt

Term.description: Chemical free-energy density term in the total free energy.
Term.evidence: where fch(ci) is the Helmholtz free energy density.

Term.description: Interfacial gradient-energy term controlling diffuse-interface energetics and anisotropy.
Term.evidence: fgrad(n)= 1/2 κ∇n·κ∇n is the gradient energy density

Term.description: Electrostatic contribution to the total free energy.
Term.evidence: felec(ci,u)= FuΣzici is the electrostatic energy density

Term.description: Fluctuation term used to represent non-uniform interface effects and generate realistic dendrite morphologies.
Term.evidence: fns(n)= h'(n)vw is the noise term presenting the non-uniform interface.

Term.expression: Butler–Volmer kinetic contribution in the phase-field evolution equation
Term.description: Electrochemical reaction term coupling phase evolution to Butler–Volmer kinetics.
Term.evidence: Coupled with Butler–Volmer kinetics, the partial differential equations that describe the temporal evolution of order parameter n can be deduced

Term.description: Combined diffusion and migration contribution in Li-ion transport.
Reaction areal current density under galvanostatic constraint.
Mass-transfer areal current density under galvanostatic constraint.
Term.evidence: Imt = F/S ∫S n·(DLi+∇cLi+ + DLi+ cLi+ F/RT ∇ul)dS

Parameter.description: Phase-field order parameter distinguishing electrolyte and lithium metal phases.
Parameter.evidence: Here, a phase field order parameter n is introduced to describe the phase change evolution of the two phases, where n = 0 denotes the electrolyte liquid phase, and n = 1 denotes the lithium metal solid phase.
The partial differential equation that describe the temporal evolution of Li ion concentration is given as

Parameter.description: Electrostatic potential in the electrolyte liquid phase.
Parameter.evidence: The electric potential in the electrolyte liquid phase ul can be solved with Poisson’s equation

Parameter.description: Electrostatic potential in the lithium metal solid phase.
Parameter.evidence: The electrostatic potential in the lithium metal solid phase us here is considered equal everywhere in the whole lithium metal solid phase

Parameter.description: Activation overpotential driving the electrochemical reaction.
Parameter.evidence: g = us − ul − Eeq is the activation overpotential

Parameter.description: Applied constant areal current density under galvanostatic operation.
Parameter.evidence: I0 is the constant areal current density as an input parameter of the model

Parameter.description: Diffusion coefficient of Li ions in electrolytes.
Parameter.evidence: where DLi+ is the Li ion diffusion coefficient in electrolytes.

Parameter.description: System temperature used in the model equations.
Parameter.evidence: R is the molar gas constant. T is the temperature.

Parameter.description: Gradient energy coefficient associated with surface-energy anisotropy.
Parameter.evidence: the gradient coefficient κ can be expanded as κ(θ)= κ0[1 + δ cos(ωθ)]

Parameter.description: Reaction constant in the phase-field evolution equations.
Parameter.evidence: where Lr denotes the interfacial mobility, Lg denotes the reaction constant.

Parameter.description: Interfacial mobility parameter in the phase-field evolution equations.
Parameter.evidence: where Lr denotes the interfacial mobility

Parameter.description: Reference potential used to distinguish active lithium from dead lithium.
Parameter.evidence: the reference potential ud is set as 1.0 mV

Parameter.description: Surface area of lithium that remains electronically connected and electrochemically active.
Parameter.evidence: when dead lithium is formed, the surface area of active lithium will suddenly reduce

Parameter.description: Total voltage/polarization of the half-cell model during stripping.
Parameter.evidence: the total voltage in the figure corresponds to the total polarization.

Parameter.description: Capacity associated with dead lithium formed during stripping.
Parameter.evidence: In the capacity curves, the dead lithium capacity begins to appear after 250.5 s and gradually rises until 404.8 s

Parameter.description: Coulombic efficiency calculated from the simulated stripping/plating process.
Parameter.evidence: The Coulombic efficiency can be directly calculated as 83.1%

Parameter.description: Simulation time during plating and stripping evolution.
Parameter.evidence: when no dead lithium is formed at 120.0 s, the average activation overpotential is only about 2 mV.

Condition.evidence: The galvanostatic simulation conditions are realized by introducing global equations... I0 is the constant areal current density as an input parameter of the model, taking positive and negative values during the charging and discharging process, respectively.
Dead lithium formation process at 0, 240.0, and 404.8 s (cut-off time), with a stripping current density of 10 mA cm2.
Lithium dendrite growth at 0, 240.0, and 480.0 s, respectively, with a plating current density of 10 mA cm2
Among them, the first piece of dead lithium is formed at 250.5 s.

Effect.evidence: when dead lithium is formed, the surface area of active lithium will suddenly reduce

Effect.magnitude: from about 2 mV to over 10 mV
Effect.evidence: when no dead lithium is formed at 120.0 s, the average activation overpotential is only about 2 mV. When a small amount of dead lithium is formed at 280.0 s, the average activation overpotential increases to about 4 mV. When a large amount of dead lithium is formed at 360.0 s, the active surface area is greatly reduced, and the activation overpotential increases to over 10 mV.
Furthermore, the Li ion concentration around the lithium dendrites will increase rapidly during the lithium stripping process

When dead lithium is formed, it will significantly strengthen the battery polarization, especially at the end of the discharge process, where a tiny dead lithium formation will also greatly increase the polarization.
In the capacity curves, the dead lithium capacity begins to appear after 250.5 s and gradually rises until 404.8 s
The Coulombic efficiency can be directly calculated as 83.1%
